words: SAM SCHRAGER: This conversation with Beulah Dollar Herrmann took place
at her home in Troy, Idaho on November 9, 1976. The interviewer
was Sam Schrager.

speaker: SS
words: When you came, where were you coming from?

speaker: BDH
words: We came from Colorado out here.

speaker: SS
words: And what was it that made you decide?

speaker: BDH
words: Well, my husband wanted to get out on a farm someplace; we were

speaker: BDH
words: living on a farm in Colorado but they had so many wind storms

speaker: BDH
words: there and it's hard on the crops and he wanted to get out here

speaker: BDH
words: and he knew some people that lived at Moscow, the Bumgardners,

speaker: BDH
words: years and years ago. I didn't know them. And he come out here

speaker: BDH
words: and looked around and he liked it-the country, so the following

speaker: BDH
words: year we moved out and we've been here ever since. And then, as

speaker: BDH
words: I said, I can't remember just when I started to work for PSychiana,

speaker: BDH
words: must have been about 1943 or 1944 that I worked

speaker: BDH
words: there.

speaker: BDH
words: Had you worked before you worked there and worked around in Mos

speaker: BDH
words: cow?

speaker: BDH
words: No, I hadn't. No, I just worked there because the type of workabout

speaker: BDH
words: the only type of work that they had around Moscow for wo

speaker: BDH
words: men was working sorting the peas and so many people couldn't do

speaker: BDH
words: that, because they ran on a belt and you couldn't do it. And

speaker: BDH
words: working-

speaker: SS
words: Like, they couldn't do it because it was hard?

speaker: BDH
words: It made them sick, you know, it was a movement all the time,

speaker: BDH
words: you see. And that's why so many of them worked for Dr. Robinson

speaker: BDH
words: because it was the type of work they could do. It wasn't hard,

speaker: BDH
words: it was a little bit confining and sometimes monotonous, but then

speaker: BDH
words: it was a job, and as I said, he was very nice to work for. Of

speaker: BDH
words: course, he very seldom come around to pay too much attention to

speaker: BDH
words: the work but then, of course, we always saw him. His office

speaker: BDH
words: was in the building where I worked and as I said, I saw Dr. Bach

speaker: BDH
words: when he was there. He was there two or three times while he was

speaker: BDH
words: in Moscow and Dr. Robinson brought him out and introduced him to

speaker: BDH
words: all of us. And then after this book come out, why, he gave each

speaker: BDH
words: of us a book. But, now, I think that you-

speaker: SS
words: You were saying that most of the people that worked there had a

speaker: SS
words: very positive impression of him-like, thought he was a good per

speaker: SS
words: son.

speaker: BDH
words: Well, all of us that worked there definitely felt, as I said, he

speaker: BDH
words: was very nice. It was more the townpeople that were more or less

speaker: BDH
words: down on him. But he never hurt anyone. He never did and he done

speaker: BDH
words: a lot of good for the town. He's the one that put the post office

speaker: BDH
words: on a first class rate; he did that because of all of his mailing.

speaker: BDH
words: See, I worked in the mailing department-we11, all of our building

speaker: BDH
words: was advertisement. We sent out the mail for these lessons and

speaker: BDH
words: then when the lessons-anyone that wanted to fill in the cards

speaker: BDH
words: and send them back, they went to the other building there and

speaker: BDH
words: they mailed the lessons out. But anytime there was a fund raising

speaker: BDH
words: deal in Moscow and they were short, Dr. Robinson filled it out.

speaker: BDH
words: But it was never published because he didn't care for that pub

speaker: BDH
words: licity. But, as I said, most of us knew it, but the people in

speaker: BDH
words: town never give him credit for anything, never. There were a

speaker: BDH
words: lot of people in town that-now like the fellow in this one drug

speaker: BDH
words: store there, he mentions him in one of his books; the Corner Drug.

speaker: BDH
words: He helped him when he first began getting started there. There

speaker: BDH
words: was a few of 'em that put money in that had, well, that had a lit

speaker: BDH
words: tle interest in it and they got their money back and everything.

speaker: BDH
words: But as time went on, why, he didn't need any help, this was when

speaker: BDH
words: he first started. But, as I said, Dr. Robinson never hurt any

speaker: BDH
words: body and you could get alot of good out of his lessons if you

speaker: BDH
words: wanted to follow through with them. Course, now, none of us

speaker: BDH
words: working there ever had them because that was one regulation thatand

speaker: BDH
words: very seldom did any of us ever read the advertisements, be

speaker: BDH
words: cause, of course, they changed from time to time, but in the hours

speaker: BDH
words: that we worked we weren't allowed to be reading it. We were sup

speaker: BDH
words: posed to be working and if some of us wanted to check some of it

speaker: BDH
words: home, we did. Iused to take some of it home, but it's been so

speaker: BDH
words: long ago I don't even remember what it was.

speaker: SS
words: But you couldn't take the lessons home and look at them?

speaker: SS
words: Well, now the lessons were put out from the other building, whether

speaker: SS
words: any of them had ever taken any of them home, Iwouldn't know. But

speaker: SS
words: in our mail it was a regulation, because we mailed thousands and

speaker: SS
words: thousands of letters a day, and see, we had to sort them just like

speaker: SS
words: they do in the post office. And if there was any for Moscow, we

speaker: SS
words: threw them out. Moscow and Pullman and towns around close, we

speaker: SS
words: threw them out, that was a regulation because, you see, he knew

speaker: SS
words: that the people of Moscow didn't go along with him or his beliefs,

speaker: SS
words: so he didn't bother them.

speaker: SS
words: I guess the one thing that Iheard was that he was afraid that

speaker: SS
words: he would be accused of trying to take people away from the other

speaker: SS
words: denominations. Yes, that was part of it. Like Isaid, he never interfered with

speaker: SS
words: any of the churches up there or anything else, but, as Isaid,

speaker: SS
words: he just didn't bother 'em at all. They were a well thought of

speaker: SS
words: family and Iknew all the family very well.

speaker: SS
words: But, he discouraged you-the people that worked for him from looking

speaker: SS
words: at his lessons or from being apart of the religion? He didn't

speaker: SS
words: really want the people-

speaker: SS
words: He never did tell us that, but then the supervisors, you know,

speaker: SS
words: always when they hired anyone as arule, why, lots of the girls

speaker: SS
words: would come in there, knew when they hired them but sometimes they

speaker: SS
words: didn't tell 'em that, but every once in awhile you'd see

speaker: SS
words: them reading it and any of us that had worked for awhile we'd

speaker: SS
words: just go and tell 'em that to just go ahead with the business in

speaker: SS
words: hand, what they were supposed to do, they weren't supposed- if

speaker: SS
words: they wanted to read them they could go ahead and take them home

speaker: SS
words: with them with them, not while they was working. And, of course,

speaker: SS
words: if one of the supervisors seen it, why, she always come over and

speaker: SS
words: bawled 'em out and the rest of us would do this on the sly to keep

speaker: SS
words: 'em from getting a bawling out.

speaker: SS
words: How were the supervisors to work for? And what were the working

speaker: SS
words: conditions like? How much was expected of you as far as output

speaker: SS
words: and all that?

speaker: SS
words: Well, they expected us to an awful lot. If we put out 35,000 in

speaker: SS
words: a day they thought we should put out about 40 or 50,000 in aday.

speaker: SS
words: Of course, that always depended on how many of us was working on

speaker: SS
words: the mailer, because you could only do so much of that. But, then

speaker: SS
words: one of the supervisors is still living, she lives here in Moscow

speaker: SS
words: on8$es Street, Jennie Nesbit. If you wanted to of course her

speaker: SS
words: husband isn't very well and so I don't know if she-if you stop

speaker: SS
words: there sometime-but then she is still living and she is the one that

speaker: SS
words: I worked for and under all the time I worked there. Of course,

speaker: SS
words: there were some others that worked closer to hertoo, you see.if

speaker: SS
words: she couldn't be there., there was other supervisors that would take

speaker: SS
words: it over.

speaker: SS
words: She was in charge of the whole operation?

speaker: BH
words: No, she was in charge of our building-

speaker: SS
words: Which was?

speaker: BDH
words: See, in the back of our building was where the-all the machines

speaker: BDH
words: was, where they done all the printing and the folding. And then

speaker: BDH
words: they were brought in from there into our part of the building and

speaker: BDH
words: we had to, oh, most of the time we had four or five different fol

speaker: BDH
words: ding things that we put in an envelope and then, you see, they

speaker: BDH
words: were put in these envelopes that were-already had the names on 'en

speaker: BDH
words: and then when we got so many hundreds and hundreds of 'em, why,

speaker: BDH
words: part of us had to go to work on the mailers and sort 'em all out.

speaker: BDH
words: And it had to be done and got to the post office each day so they

speaker: BDH
words: could be sent out. And we run them through those mailers, as I

speaker: BDH
words: said, lots of times Iknow that we mailed as high as 65 and 70,003

speaker: BDH
words: aday. Of course, we really had to work fast to do it. But then

speaker: BDH
words: lots of times we didn't put that many out because sometimes where

speaker: BDH
words: we had to change-each groUp of names went by numbers and when you

speaker: BDH
words: got those filled, why, you started on another group and they all

speaker: BDH
words: had to be kept separate, you see, until they were put into the

speaker: BDH
words: mail. So that way when the cards were numbered that were put in

speaker: BDH
words: there, they'd come back to the other building and they could tell

speaker: BDH
words: what percent of acertain group of advertising names-what percent

speaker: BDH
words: they got back.

speaker: SS
words: And what was your own job? What were you doing?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I did a little bit of all in that part, of course, I never

speaker: SS
words: worked in the part of the building where the machinery was. We

speaker: SS
words: had-I stamped cards, as I said, they had to have each card that

speaker: SS
words: you filled out-but each group of advertising names that you got

speaker: SS
words: like they come from this company in New York, this company in

speaker: SS
words: Chicago or someplace else, they were kept separate and if they

speaker: SS
words: were 40,000 of them names, you stamped 40,000 cards with that num

speaker: SS
words: ber on them. And that was one of my jobs, but you see, you would

speaker: SS
words: get tired of doing that, of course, there was only a few of us

speaker: SS
words: that could stamp those cards because you had to do it fast. Well,

speaker: SS
words: and then, as I said, we had these stacks of folded material in

speaker: SS
words: front of us-sometimes it was three or four and then the card and

speaker: SS
words: we put 'em together like this and put 'em in an envelope and then

speaker: SS
words: we put them in these-boxes and when you got these boxes full,they

speaker: SS
words: were about this size, they went to the mailer and they had to be

speaker: SS
words: run through the mailer.

speaker: SS
words: And the information in those envelopes would be just ageneralmostly

speaker: SS
words: advertisements for Psychiana?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, and the card then, if you wanted the lessons, you filled the

speaker: SS
words: card out and mailed them back and they went right up to the Other

speaker: SS
words: building.

speaker: SS
words: Right.

speaker: BDH
words: And they sent the lessons out, this other building.

speaker: SS
words: These lists that he got, were they from-how did he get them? Were

speaker: SS
words: they from organizations or where would they come from?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes, a lot of people yet today wonder how they get an advertise

speaker: BDH
words: ment in the mail.

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: BDH
words: Well, it's very easy for me to know, because that was one of my

speaker: BDH
words: jobs. I opened up the boxes by the hundreds and hundreds. One

speaker: BDH
words: advertisement company will sell them to maybe, 5000,other com

speaker: BDH
words: panies. Like if there's an ad in the paper for something regard

speaker: BDH
words: less of what it is, you know how many people may send in for itthey've

speaker: BDH
words: got your name on the mailing list. They sell that list

speaker: BDH
words: of names, as I said, if there's 5,000 other companies that want

speaker: BDH
words: 'em, they sell 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have any idea of where his lists were coming form?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes, I knew where they all came from. As I said, it was just may

speaker: BDH
words: be everywhere, maybe it was somebody that was advertising some

speaker: BDH
words: foolish, ridiculous thing to sell, like I said, Dr. Robinson what

speaker: BDH
words: Dr. Robinson put out didn't hurt anybody where some of this

speaker: BDH
words: stuff-where they got the names at wasn't-

speaker: SS
words: I wonder like if he got the lists from seed companies or some

speaker: SS
words: place like that

speaker: BDH
words: Yes. Yes, any place.

speaker: SS
words: Anywhere he could then?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes, any advertisement that you see in any magazine, they have

speaker: BDH
words: the right to sell those names and they do sell 'em, they sell

speaker: BDH
words: 'em to hundreds and hundreds of advertisers. And yet, today, now

speaker: BDH
words: like you can get something in the mail that you didn't send for

speaker: BDH
words: or anything and you wonder where it come from. That's where it

speaker: BDH
words: come from-it was sold by someplace that has your name.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when we talked a little about the kind of people who would

speaker: SS
words: be interested in the lessons; did you have any idea yourself of

speaker: SS
words: who would be the people who were asking for Psychiana?

speaker: BDH
words: Well, I never met any of them, no. But I think they were from

speaker: BDH
words: everyplace else in the United States and in the world. You see,

speaker: BDH
words: we mailed a lot of them to different places in Europe, different

speaker: BDH
words: islands and as I said, no, I never met any of the people.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that a great part of the people who were getting

speaker: SS
words: the material were from the big cities?

speaker: BDH
words: What?

speaker: SS
words: Were from the big cities? Do you think many of the people

speaker: BDH
words: An awful lot of 'em, yes, an awful lot of 'em. But, of course,

speaker: BDH
words: not all. But they had to be, you could tell that because now

speaker: BDH
words: like when we went through the list of New York City names, you

speaker: BDH
words: know, why, that part of the mail would be full, five or six times

speaker: BDH
words: a day or Chicago or San Francisco or all like that. But then, of

speaker: BDH
words: course they were from little town, too. Because in sorting your

speaker: BDH
words: mail unless you had ten from one town they had to go separately,

speaker: BDH
words: you know. So, as I said-but it's been a good many years since I

speaker: BDH
words: worked there and a lot of it I've forgot.

speaker: SS
words: What makes you figure that the lessons-what's your own personal

speaker: SS
words: knowledge to feel that the lessons were good? For people.

speaker: BDH
words: I think so because it's like I say, they wouldn't hurt anybody

speaker: BDH
words: if you wanted to follow through with them and there was a course

speaker: BDH
words: of lessons, I can't remember any more how many, they were sent

speaker: BDH
words: and then they were followed up and anytime if they wanted to dis

speaker: BDH
words: continue it they could, or if they wanted their money back they

speaker: BDH
words: got it back, that is, if they hadn't got all the lessons. But

speaker: BDH
words: then outside of that that's all I really know about the lessons

speaker: BDH
words: because that wasn't in our department.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know what the cost of them was?

speaker: BDH
words: No, I used to know, yes, but I

speaker: SS
words: I ran across in the literature-

speaker: BDH
words: No, I don't think you would in any of that. Now, you might in

speaker: BDH
words: some of this literature because Lonnie said that he knew that all

speaker: BDH
words: of his writings and literature that he had at home after he pas

speaker: BDH
words: sed away-you see, then she moved from the house soon afterwards

speaker: BDH
words: and he knew that it was turned over to the University (Idaho) and

speaker: BDH
words: some of the books that he thought hadn't been put out or some

speaker: BDH
words: thing like that. He said all of that was turned over there and

speaker: BDH
words: he said at that time when I told him about you, why, he said

speaker: BDH
words: "I'd like to talk to him." And I said, "Well, you bring the

speaker: BDH
words: books up," and I said, "we'll give him a ring." But as I said,

speaker: BDH
words: you know, he kept forgetting to bring it up because he was busy

speaker: BDH
words: working down there.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what was the pay like that you got? Do you remember?

speaker: BDH
words: Not very much because that was during the Depression that I wor

speaker: BDH
words: ked there. And when I started in I was geltjng sixty some dollars

speaker: BDH
words: amonth but that was pretty good pay at that time, you know. And,

speaker: BDH
words: of course, we got afew raises but there was one thing that he

speaker: BDH
words: never done, if we were out afew days sick we were never docked

speaker: BDH
words: our pay

speaker: SS
words: Oh, really, you got paid for those days?

speaker: BDH
words: We got paid for it, yes, we did. But, as I said,: that was during

speaker: BDH
words: hard times and it isn't like your salaries now, but that was pret

speaker: BDH
words: ty good pay for us. Because now, the girls that did the typing,

speaker: BDH
words: they got more pay and of course the supervisors got more pay.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have breaks in your work day?

speaker: BDH
words: Fifteen minutes was all.

speaker: SS
words: How often?

speaker: BDH
words: Just once a day.

speaker: SS
words: That all?

speaker: BDH
words: Once aday because that haft been changed because at that time

speaker: BDH
words: people didn't have coffee breaks all day long.

speaker: SS
words: So that fifteen minutes for lunch?

speaker: BDH
words: No. Well, we had an hour at lunch, but we had fifteen minutes, we

speaker: BDH
words: could either take it in the morning or we could take it in the

speaker: BDH
words: afternoon and only two of us went out together and that was it.

speaker: SS
words: You mean the others kept working?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes. The others kept working.

speaker: SS
words: You went off in pairs?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes. But as Isaid, it isn't like it is-like it has been for the

speaker: BDH
words: past twenty years-coffee breaks all the time.

speaker: SS
words: Right. You were expected to put out alot while you were working?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes, we were.

speaker: SS
words: You worked very fast.

speaker: BDH
words: Yes we were expected to put out alot and we did. What Imean is

speaker: BDH
words: we like some days where there was achange over with alot of

speaker: BDH
words: different numbers or-of course, we didn't always run with as

speaker: BDH
words: many, sometimes we-there'd be maybe twenty of us working and

speaker: BDH
words: then there'd be kind of aslack period and part of 'em'd be laid

speaker: BDH
words: off, but there has been as many as forty-five or fifty working

speaker: BDH
words: in the two buildings.

speaker: BDH
words: Does that include the people working on the printing as well?

speaker: BDH
words: Yes. Of course, as arule there was only four in the printing

speaker: BDH
words: but then they run those all day long, the printing machines and

speaker: BDH
words: the folding machines, you know. And alot of the girls that work

speaker: BDH
words: ed there, they were girls that were putting their husbands

speaker: BDH
words: through school, through the university and they had very little to

speaker: BDH
words: live on, you know, but they were glad to have the work while they

speaker: BDH
words: were there.

speaker: SS
words: Eight hours a day five days a week?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, that's what it was.

speaker: SS
words: Were some of the people-were many of the people also local peo

speaker: SS
words: ple like yourself?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, they were all local people, right there. Afew of the girls

speaker: SS
words: came from Deary and afew of 'em from Troy and once in awhile

speaker: SS
words: you got.-some from Pullman, but most all of 'em were local people

speaker: SS
words: right here. Yeah, they all were and they were all glad- Iknow

speaker: SS
words: that one girl that worked there-her small brother was killed out

speaker: SS
words: on the highway and the mother was awidow and they didn't have

speaker: SS
words: very much and Ido know that he paid all the funeral expenses and

speaker: SS
words: everything. But the people in town never knew that; they didn't

speaker: SS
words: know that. As I said, he didn't want them to know those things.

speaker: SS
words: And, as I said, other than that I do know that when they were

speaker: SS
words: short in funds, when they were raising funds for things and they

speaker: SS
words: were short, why, he made it up. But he never got any credit for

speaker: SS
words: it.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think of the people who disliked him in Moscow? Why

speaker: SS
words: do you think they didn't-there was so much of this giving him a

speaker: SS
words: hard time?

speaker: BDH
words: That would be only in my own opinion; so many of them-Moscow is

speaker: BDH
words: a town that is a little bit hard to get used to. I've always

speaker: BDH
words: felt that-years and years back they always felt a little above

speaker: BDH
words: most other towns or something, why I didn't know. But I think

speaker: BDH
words: most of 'em were just more or less down on him because they didn't

speaker: BDH
words: believe what he was doing. But if you pinned 'em right down to

speaker: BDH
words: it, they didn't even know what he was doing. And as I said, Most

speaker: BDH
words: of it- of course, I very seldom ever talked to anybody about it,

speaker: BDH
words: because, if anybody, if I was around somebody and they started

speaker: BDH
words: saying something about him I'd always just say, "Well, now let's

speaker: BDH
words: drop it right here I work for Dr. Robinson and he's a very fine

speaker: BDH
words: man to work for and you don't know anything about what you're

speaker: BDH
words: talking about." And I know he set up a youth center up there.

speaker: BDH
words: There was no place for the younger school kids to go to and he

speaker: BDH
words: put up this youth center, every bit of it; he paid for every bit

speaker: BDH
words: of it and paid to have it kept open and paid to have someone stay

speaker: BDH
words: there. He did that.

speaker: SS
words: Was it free to the kids? To use.

speaker: BDH
words: Yes, it was free for the kids. He did that and another thing -

speaker: BDH
words: where Robinson's Lake is at Moscow, that he- he gave that to the

speaker: BDH
words: county and for years there was never anything much done about it

speaker: BDH
words: they didn't try to keep it up. But now, in the past ten or so

speaker: BDH
words: years they have tried to do something with it. Of course, it's

speaker: BDH
words: like a lot of other parks and things they try to fix up something

speaker: BDH
words: and then it's destroyed. But now, Dr. Robinson did that, too.

speaker: BDH
words: And I don't know of anybody else around town thats did those

speaker: BDH
words: things!

speaker: SS
words: Anything like that. That's right.

speaker: BDH
words: And as I said, that is my opinion and it was most everyone's

speaker: BDH
words: opinion; course, we ourselves that worked there, we didn't dis

speaker: BDH
words: cuss those things, you know.

speaker: SS
words: It's interesting what you say about Moscow being a little cold

speaker: SS
words: because other people who came to Moscow in the twenties have told

speaker: SS
words: me the same thing. It was not an easy town to be accepted in.

speaker: SS
words: No it isn't. And we lived up here twenty some years-twenty-three

speaker: SS
words: years and so I felt that I, you know, as I say, was really on that's

speaker: SS
words: the way Moscow is. Of course, now in the past ten or

speaker: SS
words: fifteen or twenty years there's been lots of other people come in

speaker: SS
words: but believe me, a lot of those older people up there are still

speaker: SS
words: just like that. They are not a bit friendly, they never were, and

speaker: SS
words: as I said, they were against Dr. Robinson, as I said, of course

speaker: SS
